speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I’ve been feeling a bit fatigued lately and had some shortness of breath when climbing stairs.

speaker: doctor
text: Thanks for coming in. Let’s start with your vitals — your diastolic blood pressure today is 68 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: And your systolic blood pressure is 136 mmHg — so overall, BP is 136 over 68.

speaker: patient
text: That’s lower than usual for me — I usually run around 140 over 85 at home.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms, ECG, and prior angiogram, we’re confirming a diagnosis of Coronary Artery Disease.

speaker: patient
text: Right — I remember the stent placement last year. Has anything new come up?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — we’re continuing Metformin 500mg twice daily to support metabolic control, especially given your borderline glucose trends.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been taking it consistently, though I did skip a dose two days ago because I felt nauseated.

speaker: doctor
text: Noted. Let’s also review your labs — your hemoglobin came back at 86 g/L, which is below normal.

speaker: patient
text: That’s concerning — I’ve been feeling unusually pale and dizzy on standing.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure just rechecked at 86 mmHg — quite a jump from the 68 we saw earlier. Was there any change in position or activity between readings?

speaker: patient
text: I stood up quickly before the second reading — maybe that’s why it spiked? But my home cuff gave me 68 again this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s helpful context. We’ll monitor closely — orthostatic variation can be significant in CAD patients, especially with anemia.

speaker: patient
text: Anemia? Is that related to the low hemoglobin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — and your repeat hemoglobin today is now 80 g/L, down from 86 — suggesting progression we need to investigate further.

speaker: patient
text: Down again? That’s worrying. Could it be from the Metformin? I read it can affect B12.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s a valid concern — and given the drop and your symptoms, I’m going to switch you from Metformin to Empagliflozin 10mg once daily, which is safer for renal and hematologic parameters.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — will that help with the fatigue too?

speaker: patient
text: By the way, I brought in my home log — that 68 mmHg diastolic reading this morning matches the one you got first thing.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you — we’ll use that consistency to guide our management. Let’s order ferritin, B12, folate, and a reticulocyte count, and schedule a cardiology follow-up in two weeks.
